planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; sanguine coneflower is native to the dry, open pine savannas and sandy, open woodlands of eastern Texas, Louisiana, Arkansas, and Oklahoma.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; sanguine coneflower is a rare Echinacea species endemic to the piney woods of eastern Texas, Louisiana, and neighboring states, producing attractive coneflowers with pale lavender to rose-pink ray petals and the characteristic spiny orange-brown central cone. Named sanguinea (blood-red) for the reddish coloration of the cone at certain stages, it is closely related to and sometimes confused with E. purpurea. The species is adapted to the sandy, slightly acidic soils and mild winters of the East Texas piney woods ecosystem, making it better adapted than many coneflowers to the conditions of zone 9a. An important pollinator plant attracting numerous native bees and monarch butterflies. The seeds are consumed by goldfinches and other finches in fall.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination in well-drained, calcareous to slightly acidic, lean, dry soil in full sun; drooping prairie coneflower grows naturally in dry prairies and open roadsides from Texas to Louisiana and the south-central US.

directions: Plant in well-drained, calcareous to slightly acidic, lean, dry soil in full sun; drooping prairie coneflower is a Gulf Coastal Plain relative of the familiar Mexican hat (R. columnifera) and tall coneflower (R. pinnata), producing bright yellow ray petals that characteristically droop or reflex downward around the prominent, elongated central cone — the same growth form that makes all ratibidas distinctive. It is adapted to the calcareous, thin, rocky or sandy soils and harsh summer heat of the Gulf South, making it more heat-tolerant than R. pinnata. The flowers attract numerous native bees and the seed heads provide winter food for goldfinches and sparrows. An excellent, low-water native for dry, sunny, prairie-style gardens and roadsides in zone 9a.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; large poppy mallow grows in dry to moist, open, sandy or clay soils of the southeastern Coastal Plain from Georgia to Texas, often appearing in disturbed roadsides and open fields.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; large poppy mallow is a spreading to erect native perennial of the southeastern Coastal Plain with deeply lobed leaves and large (to 2.5 inches), vivid magenta-pink to wine-red, cup-shaped flowers with white centers — the same brilliantly colored poppy-mallow flowers that make the related Callirhoe involucrata so popular in gardens. This southeastern species is better adapted to the heat and humidity of zone 9a than many of its western relatives. The flowers open in the morning and close by afternoon — best enjoyed in the morning hours. Native bees, particularly long-tongued bumblebees and miner bees, are important pollinators of the nectar-rich flowers. Drought tolerant once established thanks to a deep, fleshy taproot.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, calcareous to neutral, dry, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; finger poppy mallow grows naturally in dry, open prairies and rocky, calcareous outcrops of the south-central US from Texas to Kansas.

directions: Plant in well-drained, calcareous to neutral, dry to moderately moist, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; finger poppy mallow is a distinctive perennial winecup with deeply cut, finger-like leaf lobes that give it its common name — the narrow, finger-like divisions distinguish it from other Callirhoe species. The vivid magenta-red, cup-shaped flowers are typical of the genus. It grows naturally in dry, calcareous prairies and rocky outcrops from Texas to Kansas, tolerating alkaline soils and summer drought well once the deep taproot is established. Like all Callirhoe, it develops a large, fleshy, turnip-like taproot that stores water and nutrients, making established plants very long-lived and drought resistant. An excellent native for xeric, prairie-style gardens and calcareous soil landscapes in the south-central US.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, calcareous to neutral, dry, rocky soil in full sun; pale poppy mallow is native to the dry, calcareous prairies and cedar glades of the south-central and central US, distinguished from other winecups by its lighter pink to nearly white flowers.

directions: Plant in well-drained, calcareous to neutral, dry, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; pale poppy mallow is a lower-growing, softer-colored winecup species with flowers in shades of pale pink to almost white — a softer, more delicate palette than the intense magenta-wine of most Callirhoe. The plant is native to dry, calcareous prairies, cedar glades, and rocky outcrops of the central US. Its lower stature (8–18 inches) makes it useful as a front-of-border wildflower or rock garden perennial. Like other Callirhoe, it develops a deep, fleshy taproot that makes it drought tolerant once established but difficult to transplant when mature. The pale flowers are produced from spring through summer, providing a long season of soft color. A charming and unusual alternative to the more commonly grown deep-colored winecups.
